title: Lesson 302
transcript: We are in the security management domain and one of the really important things that companies have to focus on from a security management perspective is how their information and anything of value is protected if that company is dealing around the world. So this section, as you can see here, deals with how companies need to understand the issues that pertain to information security in a global context. And so that includes understanding computer crime, but also how we protect our own valuable information assets by using some intellectual property laws that can give companies the protection that they may need for those very valuable informational assets. Let's start with why is it so important to do this? There's been a couple of interesting studies recently, and I'm sure you've heard anecdotally, like cybercrime's a pretty big deal these days, but here was one study that I really liked. A European Union agency in Nisa They did a study in 2015. So this is getting pretty old at this point. In 2015, they did a study where they looked at how profitable is the global drug trade. So if you look at every type of drug that's out there traded anywhere in the world, how profitable is that versus how profitable is cyber crime? And in 2015, which do you think was more profitable, John? Well, I'm going to guess cybercrime. Yeah, it was, by far, which is crazy, right? Because you always see these drug dealers, you know, like, sleeping on beds of, you know, $100 bills and stuff. I mean, drug trade, wildly profitable. Cybercrime? more profitable than that in 2015 and it's only gotten stronger since then. There was an estimate done about a year ago by the FBI that estimated that the profit from cybercrime globally was about 1.5 trillion. Huge, huge. It's wildly profitable for cyber criminals to be in this business. So what do corporations, what do organizations need to do to protect themselves? How do organizations deal with this, John? Well, they have to keep an eye on what's happening from a cybercrime perspective, look at the trends, look at how these people are actually getting in and making all that money, so that part of our security management is going to be focused on protecting against cybercrimes. And let's face it, it's what companies today are calling, what, cybersecurity? Which is only really one aspect of what security management needs to look at. And you're understanding that basically. Perfect example of trends that are happening right now, ransomware. Very profitable. And this highlights an important point. The attackers only need to find one flaw, whereas we as defenders have to try and protect everything. But you can't protect everything. No. And I think we've talked about that, right? Yeah. I mean, the whole focus of security is not to prevent something 100%. That's not possible. But what you want to do is what it says on your slide here. you can't prevent everything so the perspective of security is to make it not worthwhile too time consuming or too expensive for someone to try because if you do a decent job of security chances are the attackers are going to move on to some other company that doesn't do as good a job and i Unless they are specifically after you, but then even in that case, you want to make it as difficult as possible. Yeah, exactly. All right, so I guess one of the major things that most companies wanna protect, one of their most valuable assets, is their intellectual property. And so that's why we're gonna spend a couple minutes talking about some different forms of intellectual property, trade secrets, patents, copyrights, trademarks. And before we get into this, I think it's worth mentioning that these laws vary fairly significantly from country to country around the world. So you don't have to be an expert in exactly how long a patent's valid for in a certain region or anything like that, but in general terms, you should know what each of these four major types of intellectual property protection are about. yeah i think understand how these can be used by organizations to protect anything that they value as intellectual property that's why they're called intellectual property laws that can give companies protection for certain novel ideas products services that sort of thing exactly And we have a slide here that walks through an example of each of these or walks through each of these and kind of explains what's it all about, what they're about. So trade secrets, they're about protecting business information. What's that? What is business information? Well, it could be any new technology, an invention, a new product, for example, that a company comes up with. Perfect example of this would be, well, everybody knows this one. The formula for Coke. Perfect example, like business information is some type of process information that a company has developed and they don't want anyone else to find out because someone else might be able to copy that process and use it. Business information, very importantly, is not disclosed. That's why when you think, what's the perfect example of a trade secret? Think the formula for Coke. Because how long has that formula been protected for and no one knows what it is? It's been, what, close to 100 years now? Yeah, probably over 100 years. And what's important to remember here in trade secret protection, Coca-Cola does not have to disclose what that formula looks like, but they have to show that they've got really good controls in place. to protect that in the first place. And trade secret protection does not prevent a competitor from backwards engineering that formula for Coca-Cola, but it would prevent some other competitor from putting out a product that is exactly like Coca-Cola, for example, that's where the trade secret protection comes in. Yeah. And term of protection, think again Coca-Cola, it's been protected for over 100 years. So term of protection, potentially infinite. And what you're trying to protect against here is someone stealing that trade secret. Next up, we have a patent. A patent's all about some novel idea, invention, the idea being that if someone comes up with some cool new way of doing something, they should get to profit from that new way of doing something for a period of time, and then it gets released into the common good, the common... for everyone else to use. So patents have to be disclosed in a patent database. They are only for a set period of time, and you're trying to make sure that the person that invented this can make profit from it for a period of time. Yeah, perfect example of patent protection is what drug companies use to protect what they've invested heavily in research and development to come up with some drug and so that would be protected for a set period of time at the end of which would become available and that's when we have generic drugs for example that A perfect example of what drug companies do to protect their drugs, patent protection. So, copyrights, what are those about, John? Well, what's important to remember about copyrights is that It protects not the idea itself, but the expression of that idea. And so look for that keyword. And so this covers what? Anything that an artist or an author has decided to express their view on something. That's why it covers movies, books, music. That sort of thing. Because that's how that author has decided to express their view on something. We could express our view on the exact same thing. It can't be done the exact same way as someone else. Right. Obviously have to disclose it because someone needs to watch the movie, the TV show, listen to the music. It's only supposed to be for a set period of time, although Disney's trying to fix that. And the whole point of this is someone else just making the exact copy of your movie, of your song, of your whatever it is. And then the final one, trademarks. Yeah, trademarks can be anything. Color, sound, a symbol, usually used by companies to distinguish their products from other products or their companies from other companies basically. to associate something that people can think of very quickly and associate with a product or a company. So obviously it has to be disclosed. We all know what the Nike swoosh looks like, that sort of thing. It's potentially infinite term of protection, and you're trying to make sure that someone doesn't make an exact copy of your trademark so it doesn't create confusion in the marketplace. So you've got some really good examples here, I think. Yeah, to make this rather dry subject a little more interesting, I came up with some visual representations in examples of these. So remember, trade secrets, it's all about protecting some secret business idea information. And of course, this giant vault here, if you go to Atlanta, you will find that you can do a tour of the Coca-Cola facilities and they literally have a giant bank vault where they pretend to keep the formula for Coke. So that's what this picture is. This is Coca-Cola's bank vault. Trade secrets, about protecting misappropriation of information. So, I found a great example of this. There was KFC, KFC's trade secret of course is the formula for their chicken right 11 herbs and spices I believe it is so someone this guy named edge noticed that KFC was following 11 people with their with KFC's Twitter account five Spice Girls and six guys named herb 11 herbs and spices so This guy noticed it, posted it on Twitter, kind of blew up, became this big deal, and KFC wanted to recognize that someone had figured this out, and so they decided to commission a work of art. And here's the beautiful oil painting they had commissioned and sent to this guy, which is hilarious. It's this guy, Edge, riding on Colonel Sanders' shoulders here into a glorious, Hey, this looks like he's going up towards a peak. He's walking away from it. Yeah, I like our peak. Like our peak. Yeah, and it gets even better when you zoom in. Just keeps giving. All right, a patent, it's about protecting some novel idea or invention. And I wanna preface this with, I did not make this up. I scoured the US patent database So this is an arduous course. You're gonna have to work hard. So you're gonna have to keep yourself motivated. So I have found the perfect patent to keep yourself motivated. So this dude called Joe Armstrong came up with this patent here that I'm showing you. I'm just gonna leave it there. It's disturbing. Yes. Yeah, okay. Copyright, about protecting the expression of an idea. And so this is why if you, so remember there used to be this place called Blockbuster and you could rent videos there? And when you would rent this video, you'd get it home and inevitably you'd have to rewind the tape because the last jackass that rented it didn't rewind it. And when you finally played that tape, you would notice that there would be this scary message. The FBI was gonna come and kick down your door and drag you out screaming in the middle of the night if you made a copy of... the video, the movie, whatever, because this is copyright. It's the protection of the expression of an idea and that's why you always saw these scary FBI warnings. And the last one, trademarks, distinguishing color, symbol, something like that. And we've all seen examples of these, the Coke's logo, Nike swoosh, things like that. Those are trademarks. The McDonald's arches. Right, exactly. There you go.
